reasoning_model_1: Model 1 demonstrates excellent execution of the 'Non-verbal vocalization' (非语言发声) dimension. From [0.00s-1.50s], the model correctly interprets the script '咳咳……' as a paralinguistic instruction, generating realistic coughing sounds instead of reading the text literally. The coughing sounds are natural and seamlessly transition into the speech '大家都安静一下...'. The audio accurately conveys the urgency and the persona of the grandfather without any mechanical artifacts or content errors, adhering perfectly to the 'Feature Control' requirements.
reasoning_model_2: Model 2 generates the required 'Non-verbal vocalization' but exhibits a flaw in content integration. While it produces a coughing sound at the beginning [0.00s-1.00s], it subsequently reads out the character '咳' (Ke) as a spoken word at [1.00s-1.20s]. This results in a redundant and unnatural sequence (Sound of cough + Spoken 'Ke'). While the core feature (coughing) is present, the failure to suppress the literal reading of the onomatopoeia reduces the naturalness and immersion compared to a purely acted performance.
model_comparison: Comparing the two models on the dimension of 'Non-verbal vocalization', Model 1 is the clear winner. Model 1 correctly substitutes the text '咳咳' with the corresponding sound effect, resulting in a fluid and realistic performance that fits the context of a grandfather speaking. Model 2, conversely, commits a text normalization error by generating the sound *and* reading the character '咳' ('Ke'), which creates a disjointed and less professional listening experience. Model 1's interpretation is fully correct (Score 3), while Model 2 is only partially correct (Score 2) due to the redundant spoken character.
